name: Broadcast stakeholders on changes with Discord hands-free

parameters.content: =✅ Broadcast stakeholders on changes with Discord hands-free — run completed at {{ $now.toFormat('HH:mm') }}

### Receive Inbound Webhook

Entry point: listens for POST calls on `/wf08625-task`. Use the TEST url while building, switch callers to the PRODUCTION url after publishing.

Next: Map to Canonical Schema

### Map to Canonical Schema

Shapes the item: writes `taskId`, `kind`, `payload`. Keeps the schema explicit between stages.

Next: Acknowledge Fast (HTTP 202)

### Acknowledge Fast (HTTP 202)

Sends the HTTP 202 response back to the caller — keep this fast; heavy work happens after/async.

Next: Route by Event Kind

### Route by Event Kind

Routes each item to one of 4 lanes: opened, closed, reopened + fallback.

Ports: ①→ Handle 'opened' → Google Sheets · ②→ Handle 'closed' → Forward API · ③→ Handle 'reopened' → Forward API · ④→ Log Unrecognized Kind

### Handle 'opened' → Google Sheets

Upserts a row into 'Task Tracker', matched on `taskId` — re-running with the same key updates the row instead of duplicating it.

Ports: ①→ Consolidate Handling · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Google OAuth2 / service account

### Handle 'closed' → Forward API

POST https://api.example.com/v1/tasks — retries 3× with backoff on failure.

Ports: ①→ Consolidate Handling · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 API key / bearer for the target API

### Handle 'reopened' → Forward API

POST https://api.example.com/v1/tasks — retries 3× with backoff on failure.

Ports: ①→ Consolidate Handling · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 API key / bearer for the target API

### Log Unrecognized Kind

Appends a new row to 'Task Tracker' — a human-readable audit trail that doesn't require opening n8n to inspect.

Ports: ①→ Consolidate Handling · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Google OAuth2 / service account

### Consolidate Handling

Rejoins 4 branches into one stream (mode: append) so downstream logic runs once.

Next: Sort Tasks Newest First

### Sort Tasks Newest First

Orders items (newest first) so limits and digests pick the most relevant records.

Next: Send Summary to Discord

### Send Summary to Discord

Posts to a Discord channel via an incoming webhook — no bot token needed, the webhook URL on the credential is the only secret.

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Discord webhook URL

### Capture Failure Context

Shapes the item: writes `failedNode`, `errorMessage`, `workflow`. Keeps the schema explicit between stages.

Next: Escalate Failure to Slack

### Escalate Failure to Slack

Posts a message to Slack (`#projects`) — a low-latency way for the team to see automation activity without checking a dashboard.

↻ retries 3×/2s · 🔑 Slack OAuth2

# Broadcast stakeholders on changes with Discord hands-free

**Pattern:** Event Notification · **Level:** Core · **Trigger:** Webhook

## What it does
Broadcast stakeholders on changes with Discord hands-free. It fires the moment an external system calls its webhook.

## Why it exists
Manual standups is slow and error-prone; this workflow makes the process repeatable, monitored and hands-off, with failures surfaced instead of silently lost.

## Main flow
Receive Inbound Webhook → Map to Canonical Schema → Acknowledge Fast (HTTP 202) → Route by Event Kind → Handle 'opened' → Google Sheets → Consolidate Handling → Sort Tasks Newest First → …

## Use it for
- Teams that want to event notification without manual effort
- Anyone automating standups in software development
- Replacing a fragile manual process with a reliable workflow

## You get
- Saves hours of manual work each week
- Standardizes the process every time
- Responds in seconds

## Before first run
Connect credentials: API key / bearer for the target API; Discord webhook URL; Google OAuth2 / service account; Slack OAuth2.
Follow the color key: 🟦 entry · 🟪 AI · 🟫 actions · ⬜ utilities · 🟩 outputs · 🟥 error rail.
Each node has its own doc card underneath — read those before editing.

## Try it now
Sample data is pinned on the trigger — hit **Execute Workflow** to watch it flow through every node before connecting any real credentials. Unpin the trigger (right-click → Unpin) when you're ready to go live.

## Design decisions

The optional HMAC check runs before any business logic touches the payload — bad signatures are rejected at the door rather than after work has already been done with untrusted data. Acking fast (HTTP 202) and doing the real work after the response is what stops the sender's webhook delivery from timing out and re-delivering the same event.

## Practice exercises

Once it's wired up with real credentials, try:

1. Turn on signature verification and trace what a tampered payload does at the 'Signature Valid?' gate.
2. Add a new event kind to the switch and give it its own handler.
3. Remove the fast-ack step and observe how response time changes under a slow downstream write.

## 🛟 Error handling & retries

External calls retry **3× with 2s backoff**. Anything still failing exits through its node's error output into the red error rail → context is captured and the on-call channel is alerted. Set an n8n **Error Workflow** in settings as the final safety net.

## 🔐 Security

Never trust inbound payloads: the webhook path is unguessable. Add header auth or IP allow-listing for defense in depth. Secrets live in n8n credentials — never in node parameters.

## 🛠 Customize me

Quick wins: point the data nodes at your own accounts (see 🔑 lines on each card), adjust the trigger cadence, and tune thresholds/labels to your standups process. Every node card explains its role — safe to extend at any marked branch.
